metadata.summary: Build a FeatureFlagManager in Python: register typed flags, load overrides from env and config file, check is_enabled() with user context. Includes decorator and context-manager integration patterns.

content.title: Feature Flag Core Implementation
content.summary: A FeatureFlagManager registers typed FeatureFlag dataclasses, loads overrides from environment variables (FF_* prefix) and a JSON config file, and exposes is_enabled(flag_name, user_id) as the single call site. The decorator pattern (@feature_flag) and a context-manager middleware layer are the two standard integration points for web frameworks.

why.p: A centralized flag manager prevents the anti-pattern of scattered ad-hoc environment variable checks throughout the codebase. Registering flags with explicit type, default, owner, and description creates a machine-readable registry that agents can audit for stale flags, missing owners, or hardcoded defaults.

Loading overrides from both env vars (FF_FLAG_NAME=true) and a JSON config file allows the same codebase to be tested locally with specific flags, deployed with CI overrides, and managed in production via a config file — without changing source code.

list.item: Building an in-process flag manager without a SaaS dependency (self-hosted, cost-sensitive, or offline-first).
Wrapping an existing code path in a flag to enable progressive rollout of a new implementation.
Adding a kill switch to a risky third-party integration that needs instant disable without redeploy.
Integrating flag evaluation into a FastAPI or Django request lifecycle via middleware.
Teams that need targeting rules, percentage rollout, or real-time flag updates without code deploys — use a dedicated service instead (see feature-flags-services-testing).
Single env-var toggling that will never need targeting or rollout — direct os.getenv() is simpler.
Cases where flag evaluation latency must be zero — compile the flag out at build time.

section.name: core-manager
section.p: The FeatureFlagManager holds a registry of FeatureFlag dataclasses and a separate overrides dict. Overrides take precedence — they allow test fixtures and deployment config to override code-level defaults without touching flag definitions.

example.title: FeatureFlagManager with env and file overrides
example.note: Unknown flag name always returns False — never True. This ensures that a missing flag registration keeps the old behavior, not the new one.

section.name: decorator-pattern
section.p: The @feature_flag decorator is the cleanest integration point when both the new and old code paths are complete functions. It selects between the two at call time without littering if/else into the calling code.

example.title: feature_flag decorator with fallback
example.note: The decorator extracts user_id from kwargs or the first positional arg by convention. If your calling convention differs, pass user_id explicitly via kwargs.

section.name: context-manager-middleware
section.p: Evaluating all flags once at request start and storing the result in a ContextVar avoids redundant evaluations per request and makes the flag state observable in logs. The FastAPI middleware example shows the full integration.

example.title: Context manager and FastAPI middleware
example.note: Log the enabled_flags list with sample-rate at request start. During incidents, the flag state is critical for triage — which variant did this user see?

section.name: agent-gotchas
section.p: These are the most common mistakes AI agents make when implementing or modifying flag code.

antipattern.description: Agents add if flag_enabled("foo") at every call site instead of at a single decision point.
antipattern.reason: Scattered flag checks multiply branches exponentially. A flag should wrap the outermost decision, not be sprinkled inside helper functions.

antipattern.description: Generating a flag-eval client with default=True so tests pass locally.
antipattern.reason: A True default leaks the new feature to 100% of production traffic when the flag is not yet configured in the flag store.

antipattern.description: Targeting rules that pass entire user objects to the flag service.
antipattern.reason: User objects often contain PII; flag services log evaluation context. Whitelist only the attributes needed for targeting (id, tier, groups).
